metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Supply a concrete search command (e.g. `grep -rn 'exit.*[12]\|rebar review\|verify-completion' .github/ scripts/ reviewer-bot/`) and a definition of 'additive-safe' (e.g. 'consumer treats any non-zero as failure, OR explicitly handles only 0/1/2/10 and can be updated') so the enumeration and safety check are reproducible.
findings.finding: The AC 'Exit-code consumers (CI, Gerrit reviewer-bot) are enumerated in the exit-11 ADR, confirmed additive-safe, with a stated rollback' requires the executor to *discover* which CI scripts and pipeline jobs reference exit codes, then assess their safety — a 'figure out who they are and verify whether they break' investigation deferred to the executor. The plan names two consumer categories but gives no file paths, no search command, and no definition of what 'additive-safe' means in a testable sense.
findings.location: What section: 'Exit-code-11 contract' bullet; AC: 'Exit-code consumers (CI, Gerrit reviewer-bot) are enumerated in the exit-11 ADR, confirmed additive-safe, with a stated rollback'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm shape (B) wiring plan explicitly accounts for the `except Exception ... from None` at transition_close.py:174 that discards `.outcome` — specify whether the fix is (a) narrowing the except to `LLMUnavailableError` first, then re-raising with outcome, or (b) catching `CommandError` and mapping from a stored outcome, or another pattern.
findings.finding: The plan's shape (B) wiring for the completion close gate relies on reading `.outcome` off the caught `LLMUnavailableError`, but `transition_close.py` line 174 is a broad `except Exception as exc: raise CommandError(...) from None` that DISCARDS the original exception (including its `.outcome`) before any exit-11 mapping can read it. The plan cannot wire shape (B) without restructuring this except block to intercept `LLMUnavailableError` before the broad-except conversion.
findings.location: What section, shape (B) description; transition_close.py line 174
findings.suggested_fix: Add a narrower `except LLMUnavailableError as exc:` arm BEFORE the broad `except Exception` in `_completion_precheck`, read `getattr(exc, 'outcome', None)` there, and re-raise a `CommandError` subclass that carries the outcome (or raise a new typed error). The broad `except Exception` arm remains for non-LLM failures.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the code-review exit-11 site is `_review_code()` (rebar review-code) rather than `_review()` (rebar review), and confirm that `llm.review_code()` surfaces `coverage.resolution_class` from the underlying `code_review_verdict` for shape (A) mapping.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'rebar review (code-review) read[s] shape (A)' and lists `rebar review` as a site to update with exit-code mapping in `_llm_commands.py`. But `_review()` (the `rebar review` handler) calls `llm.review_ticket()` — it is a TICKET reviewer, not the code-review gate. The code-review gate CLI is `_review_code()` / `rebar review-code`. The plan appears to conflate the two, risking that implementers apply exit-11 mapping to the wrong command.
findings.location: What section: 'Sites to update, each with a CLI test: rebar review-plan (_llm_commands.py, currently 0/1/2) and rebar review (code-review) read shape (A)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate and fix the `_completion_precheck` broad-except re-wrap: either propagate `.outcome` through `CommandError` (e.g. add an `outcome` attribute to `CommandError`) or move the retryable→exit-11 mapping inside `_completion_precheck` before the re-raise, so the `.outcome` is not destroyed by `raise CommandError(...) from None`.
findings.finding: The plan's shape-B exit-11 path for the completion close gate is broken by an unenumerated intermediate re-wrap: `transition_close.py:174` has a broad `except Exception as exc: raise CommandError(...)` that converts ANY exception — including `LLMUnavailableError` with its `.outcome` attachment — into a plain `CommandError` before it can reach the CLI. The plan says the CLI 'CATCHES the exception and reads `getattr(exc, "outcome", None)`', but `LLMUnavailableError` never escapes `_completion_precheck`; the CLI only ever sees `CommandError` which carries no outcome. This is the primary seam the exit-11 mapping for the completion close gate depends on, and it is not currently enumerated or addressed in scope.
findings.location: What section / shape-B data flow; `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:174`
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `outcome` attribute to `CommandError` (or a subclass `RetryableCommandError`) so `_completion_precheck` can set `exc.outcome = original_llm_exc.outcome` before re-raising. The CLI then reads `getattr(exc, 'outcome', None)` off the `CommandError` it catches.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's CLI site identification: the code-review gate CLI is `rebar review-code` (`_review_code()` in `_llm_commands.py`), not `rebar review` (`_review()`) — update the shape-A site list and all associated AC/test references accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan names `rebar review` as the code-review gate CLI (shape A, exits 11), but `rebar review` (`_review()` in `_llm_commands.py`) calls `llm.review_ticket` (per-ticket advisory review returning `review_result`), NOT the code-review gate (`produce_code_review_verdict` returning `code_review_verdict`). The actual code-review gate CLI is `rebar review-code` (`_review_code()`). `_review_code()` currently exits 0 unconditionally on success regardless of verdict. The plan's instruction to add exit-11 to `rebar review` (code-review) would target the wrong command.
findings.location: What section: 'Sites to update, each with a CLI test: rebar review-plan (_llm_commands.py, currently 0/1/2) and rebar review (code-review) read shape (A)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'rebar review (code-review)' with 'rebar review-code (_review_code() in _llm_commands.py)' everywhere in the plan's site-update list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve how `LLMOutcome` survives through `transition_close.py`'s `CommandError` re-raise: either (a) propagate `.outcome` explicitly onto the `CommandError` (`err.outcome = getattr(original_exc, 'outcome', None)`) before re-raising, or (b) restructure the close path to let `LLMUnavailableError` propagate directly to the exit site.
findings.finding: The plan asserts (shape B) that the completion close CLI can read `getattr(exc, 'outcome', None)` off the caught exception to get the LLMOutcome, but the existing `transition_close.py:174-181` broad-catch ALREADY re-raises a new `CommandError` (losing the original `LLMUnavailableError` and its `.outcome` attribute). The plan does not address how `.outcome` survives this re-raise, making the shape-B wiring an unverified capability assertion.
findings.location: What section, 'Exit-code-11 contract — ALL THREE gate CLIs', shape (B) description; `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:174-181`
findings.suggested_fix: In `_completion_precheck`, before constructing the `CommandError`, extract and store the outcome: `_outcome = getattr(exc, 'outcome', None)`, then after `raise CommandError(...) from None` is replaced with a two-step: construct `cmd_err = CommandError(..., returncode=1)`, set `cmd_err.outcome = _outcome`, then `raise cmd_err`. The CLI exit site then reads `getattr(exc, 'outcome', None)` and finds it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the per-class actionable message string (or template) for each of the 8 `ResolutionClass` values in the plan, co-located with AC #7 or the What section, so the degrade helpers in all three gates produce consistent, specified messages.
findings.finding: The plan mandates 'per-class actionable messages' (AC #7: 'A degraded verdict carries a class-specific, actionable message naming the disposition') and lists it in the What section, but never defines the message text or template for any of the 8 ResolutionClass values. The enum vocabulary is defined in failure.py but the message-per-class mapping is absent from the plan.
findings.location: What section — 'Per-class actionable messages + exit code 11'; AC line 7
findings.suggested_fix: Add a small table in the What section mapping each ResolutionClass value to its human-facing message, e.g.: WAIT_AND_RETRY → 'Transient provider overload — wait and retry'; RETRY_NOW → 'Transient connection error — retry immediately'; INCREASE_PROVIDER_LIMITS → 'Provider quota/billing limit reached — increase limits'; CHANGE_SETTINGS → 'Invalid credentials or configuration — check settings'; CHANGE_INPUT → 'Input too large or content-filtered — reduce input size'; CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL → 'All configured providers failed — change provider or model'; FIX_AGENT_DESIGN → 'Agent step/tool budget exhausted — redesign agent prompts'; NEEDS_INVESTIGATION → 'Unknown LLM failure — see diagnostic for details'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the session-log write calling convention for the diagnostic: what is passed as `entry` (e.g., `json.dumps(diagnostic, indent=2)`), what `summary` text is used, and whether `relates_to` is set to the reviewed ticket_id.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that 'writing the sanitized diagnostic to the session log (log_session/append_session_log) is owned by THIS story' but never specifies the calling convention: what string is passed to entry:str (JSON dump of the diagnostic dict? a formatted message?), which ticket_id/relates_to is used, or at which specific code site the call is made.
findings.location: What section — 'Session-log sink ownership'; AC line 15

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a bullet-point outline (Context, Decision, Consequences) for the exit-11 ADR and the diagnostic-sanitization ADR within the plan, mirroring the level of detail provided for transport-retry and async-watchdog.
findings.finding: The plan states it will record four ADRs, two of which (exit-11 contract and diagnostic-sanitization contract) have no stubs, no content outline, and no structure defined in the plan text. The other two (transport-retry 0037, async-watchdog 0038) already exist as complete authored stubs. An executor has no guidance on required sections, rationale points, or decision content for the missing two.
findings.location: What section — 'Four ADRs are recorded: exit-11 contract, diagnostic sanitization, deferred async watchdog, transport-retry side-effect-safety architecture'; AC line 18

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `transition_close.py:174`'s broad `except Exception … from None` re-wrap must be split into a separate `except LLMUnavailableError` branch (or the `.outcome` forwarded onto the `CommandError`) so shape-B exit-11 routing can read the disposition at the CLI layer.
findings.finding: Shape B exit-11 data-flow is structurally blocked: `transition_close.py:174` catches ALL exceptions (including `LLMUnavailableError`) and re-raises as `CommandError(...) from None`, severing the exception chain. The `.outcome` attribute on the original `LLMUnavailableError` is permanently discarded. The plan's What section states 'its close-gate/CLI CATCHES the exception and reads `getattr(exc, 'outcome', None)` → `.resolution_class`', but what the CLI actually receives is a plain `CommandError(returncode=1)` with no cause and no `.outcome`. The plan names this seam ('transition_close.py:174, fail-closed re-raise') without identifying that the re-wrap must be surgically changed (e.g. peel off `LLMUnavailableError` into a separate except branch before the broad catch) for shape B to work.
findings.location: What section, 'TWO distinct data-flow shapes' / shape B; src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py line 174
findings.suggested_fix: Add a dedicated `except LLMUnavailableError as exc:` clause before the broad `except Exception` at transition_close.py:174 that re-raises a new `CommandError` carrying the outcome (e.g. `err = CommandError(..., returncode=11 if retryable else 1); err.outcome = exc.outcome; raise err from exc`) so the CLI can branch on it — OR let the `LLMUnavailableError` propagate unmodified to the CLI boundary and handle it there.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the write-site spec to address the mid-workflow fallback path (gate_dispatch.py lines 204-212) where `res.error` is a `str`, not the original `LLMUnavailableError`; specify how `.outcome` is made available (e.g. `RunResult` carries the original exception, or the executor re-attaches it, or the fallback path maps to a default resolution class).
findings.finding: Shape A write-site is unreachable in the mid-workflow degrade path: `produce_plan_review_verdict` calls `_degraded_plan_review_verdict(…, error=(res.error or '…'))` at gate_dispatch.py line 204-212, where `res.error` is typed `str | None` (`RunResult.error: str | None`). The original `LLMUnavailableError` exception — which carries the `.outcome` attribute — is not preserved on `RunResult`. The plan's write-site spec says the helper 'reads off the caught error's `getattr(exc, 'outcome', None)` (the mamba LLMOutcome)', but in this code path no exception object exists; only a string survives. The two `except LLMUnavailableError as exc:` explicit catches (lines 136, 173) do have the original exception, but the third degrade path (fallback on mid-workflow failure) does not.
findings.location: What section, 'WRITE SITE (shape A)'; src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py lines 204-212, executor.py RunResult.error

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove `coverage.resolution_class` and `coverage.diagnostic` from the `completion_verdict.schema.json` addition, since the completion gate always fails by raising (shape B) and never produces a verdict dict with coverage — those fields have no write-site on the completion path.
findings.finding: Adding `coverage.resolution_class` + `coverage.diagnostic` to `completion_verdict.schema.json` is YAGNI: the completion gate never produces a degraded verdict dict — it raises `LLMUnavailableError` (shape B), so the completion_verdict schema fields have no actual write-site and will remain permanently unset.
findings.location: Plan section: 'Verdict-schema field'; `src/rebar/schemas/completion_verdict.schema.json`; `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py:872-880` (`produce_completion_verdict`)
findings.suggested_fix: Scope the verdict-schema field additions to `plan_review_verdict.schema.json` and `code_review_verdict.schema.json` only (the shape-A verdict-dict gates). The completion_verdict schema needs no coverage additions because shape-B failures carry the `LLMOutcome` on the raised exception, not in a verdict dict.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and describe a test) for the pre-sign fingerprint re-check failure path: when material state has drifted between verify and sign, the re-check must detect the drift and raise / refuse to sign — not silently proceed.
findings.finding: The pre-sign fingerprint re-check is a new security-relevant guard, but no AC or test is specified for the failure path: when the fingerprint has drifted between verify and sign, the re-check should detect it and refuse to sign. Only the happy path (re-check passes) and the degraded-verdict rejection are covered; the drift-detected failure path has no AC or described test.
findings.location: AC: 'The material fingerprint is re-checked immediately before signing.' / Scope: 'transition_close.py (preserve verify-outside-lock ordering + add pre-sign fingerprint re-check)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'If the material fingerprint computed at pre-sign differs from the one captured at verify-time, sign_manifest raises and no attestation is written (asserted by a test that injects a drift between verify and sign).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ACs for the MCP gate's failure sub-paths: (a) non-retryable disposition returns a structured dict with a non-retryable classification (not the same as retryable), and (b) an exception with no attached LLMOutcome returns a structured failure result (not a raw raise) with a defined fallback shape.
findings.finding: The MCP structured failure result is a new user-facing flow, but no AC or test covers the failure sub-paths beyond the happy-path 'returns a structured dict carrying resolution_class.' Specifically: (a) what the MCP gate returns when the disposition is non-retryable (vs. retryable), and (b) what it returns when no outcome is attached to the exception. These are distinct observable behaviors that callers branch on.
findings.location: What section: 'MCP surface (concrete): the write-gated MCP gate tools return the failure as a structured dict carrying resolution_class + the class-specific message...' / AC: 'The write-gated MCP LLM surface returns a structured failure result (never a raw raise) carrying the disposition.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact key set of the structured MCP failure dict (e.g., `{"verdict": "INDETERMINATE", "resolution_class": "WAIT_AND_RETRY", "message": "...", "retryable": true}`) so all three gate tools return a consistent, testable shape.
findings.finding: The plan's MCP surface AC states 'the write-gated MCP LLM surface returns a structured failure result (never a raw raise) carrying the disposition' but never defines the shape/keys of that structured dict. The current MCP tools in _mcp_llm.py call rebar.llm.review_plan() / verify_completion() directly and would propagate an unhandled LLMUnavailableError as a raw exception; the plan provides no dict schema for the failure return.
findings.location: What section — 'MCP surface (concrete)'; AC line 19

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the test file and describe the assertion shape for the write-lock ordering test (e.g. 'in `tests/unit/test_transition_close.py`, assert that `verify_completion` is called before `_acquire_write_lock` in the call sequence using mock call-order inspection').
findings.finding: The AC 'Verify remains outside the write lock (ordering preserved; asserted by a test)' names a test but provides no location, no test-file path, and no assertion pattern. An executor cannot verify this AC is satisfied without knowing where the test lives or what it asserts.
findings.location: AC: 'Verify remains outside the write lock (ordering preserved; asserted by a test)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command or test description for the fingerprint-recheck AC, e.g. 'in `tests/unit/test_signing.py`, assert that `_compute_fingerprint` is called as the last operation before `_write_attestation` using mock call-order inspection, and that a fingerprint mismatch raises before the HMAC is written'.
findings.finding: The AC 'The material fingerprint is re-checked immediately before signing' has no described implementation step and no proving command. The plan mentions the requirement in the 'Never-sign' bullet but gives no test or command that would confirm the re-check occurs at the correct site in `signing.py`.
findings.location: AC: 'The material fingerprint is re-checked immediately before signing'; What section: 'Never-sign structural guard' bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling for the session-log diagnostic write: define whether a write failure should be caught-and-logged-at-WARNING (so the gate completes with the degraded verdict) or propagated, and add this to the AC for gate_dispatch.py's degrade helpers.
findings.finding: The session-log write of the sanitized diagnostic (new I/O at the degrade site, 'Session-log sink ownership' section) has no error-handling specification: if `log_session`/`append_session_log` raises (disk full, lock contention, session not found), the plan does not say whether the gate should swallow and continue, propagate and abort, or fall back — leaving an unhandled failure path at a write operation in the hot gate path.
findings.location: 'Session-log sink ownership' section; AC: 'The sanitized diagnostic is written to the session log at the degrade site'
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the `log_session`/`append_session_log` call in a try/except that logs a WARNING (e.g., `logger.warning('diagnostic sink write failed: %s', exc)`) and continues — the degraded verdict should still be returned even if the session log write fails, so the gate caller receives the resolution_class and actionable message.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify or replace the MCP 'return structured dict on failure' pattern: either cite MCP spec guidance showing structured-dict error returns are idiomatic, or use the established `isError: true` path (raise a typed exception) and include `resolution_class` in the exception message or a structured `data` field if the SDK supports it.
findings.finding: The plan proposes a novel MCP error-surface pattern where write-gated LLM tools 'return a structured dict carrying resolution_class … not an opaque error string', departing from the standard MCP tool error convention (raising exceptions that the SDK converts to `isError: true` tool results). This pattern is not grounded in MCP spec guidance or prior art in the codebase (current tools all raise `ValueError`/`LLMError` on failure); the plan provides no justification for why a structured-dict return is preferred over an `isError` tool-error with a structured body.
findings.location: What section, 'MCP surface (concrete)' bullet; `src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the never-sign guard attaches at the CALLER sites (e.g. `sign_plan_review` in `attest.py`, `_completion_precheck` in `transition_close.py`) where the verdict dict is still in scope — not inside `signing.sign_manifest` which only receives a pre-built manifest list.
findings.finding: The structural never-sign guard is mis-located: `sign_manifest(ticket_id, manifest)` in `signing.py` takes a pre-built manifest list, not a verdict dict, so it cannot inspect `verdict.verdict` or `coverage.get('resolution_class')`. The guard must live in the verdict-to-manifest callers (`_completion_precheck` / `sign_plan_review` / `close_ticket`) where the verdict object is still in scope — not in `sign_manifest` itself.
findings.location: Plan section: 'Never-sign structural guard'; `src/rebar/signing.py` `sign_manifest` function signature; `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:317`; `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:347`
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Never-sign structural guard' section, replace 'in the attestation/sign_manifest path' with 'in the callers that build the manifest from the verdict — `sign_plan_review` (attest.py) and `_completion_precheck` (transition_close.py) — before `sign_manifest` is called.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the exact injection site for the never-sign guard: either (a) add a `verdict_dict` parameter to `sign_manifest()` and add the assertion there, or (b) add a shared `assert_signable(verdict)` helper called by every signing caller (`_run_plan_review`, `close_ticket`), and name the file/function in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan's 'never-sign structural guard' AC states the guard asserts `verdict.verdict == 'PASS' and verdict.coverage.get('resolution_class') is None` BEFORE signing. The actual `sign_manifest()` function in `signing.py` accepts any manifest with no verdict-state check whatsoever. The plan does not name where in the call chain this guard will be added — `sign_manifest` itself, the `_run_plan_review` wrapper, or `transition_close.py`'s `_completion_precheck`. Without a specific injection site, different developers may add the guard in inconsistent or ineffective locations.
findings.location: Never-sign structural guard section; AC 'The structural never-sign guard rejects signing any degraded / INDETERMINATE verdict'; src/rebar/signing.py sign_manifest()

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the never-sign guard's exact attachment point: specify which function(s) in `plan_review/__init__.py` and/or `transition_close.py` receive the new `resolution_class` check, and update the Scope list to reflect the actual edit sites (not `signing.py` generically).
findings.finding: The plan says the never-sign structural guard attaches at 'the attestation/`sign_manifest` path' (implying `src/rebar/signing.py::sign_manifest`), but `sign_manifest` is a low-level HMAC utility that receives only a `ticket_id` and a `manifest` list — it has no access to the verdict dict or its `coverage.resolution_class`. The actual plan-review signing decision lives in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:482-499` where the verdict object is in scope. The described guard (`verdict.verdict == "PASS" and verdict.coverage.get("resolution_class") is None`) cannot be placed in `signing.py` without a schema-aware redesign; it belongs in `_run_plan_review` (plan-review) and in `transition_close.py:344` (completion). The Scope line says `signing.py` is the attachment point, which is inconsistent with the implementation detail.
findings.location: Never-sign structural guard section; `src/rebar/signing.py::sign_manifest` (lines 464–520); `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:482-499`
findings.suggested_fix: The guard belongs in `_run_plan_review` immediately before the `attest.sign_plan_review` call (extending the existing `if sign and verdict.get('verdict') == 'PASS' and ...` condition) and in `close_ticket` / `_verdict_manifest` before `_signing.sign_manifest` is called. `signing.py` itself does not need to change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the never-sign guard placement: name the specific call sites where the guard is added (plan_review/__init__.py signing block and/or transition_close.py pre-sign check), distinct from the low-level signing.py HMAC utility which cannot see the verdict dict.
findings.finding: The plan places the never-sign structural guard 'in the attestation/sign_manifest path' but sign_manifest in signing.py is a low-level HMAC utility that has no knowledge of verdict structure or resolution_class. The actual plan-review signing caller is plan_review/__init__.py (line ~482) and the close-gate signing caller is transition_close.py (line ~347). The plan does not specify which of these callers receives the guard.
findings.location: What section — 'Never-sign structural guard (concrete + consumer-safe)'; Scope — 'the attestation/sign_manifest path'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'in the attestation/sign_manifest path' with 'at the two plan-review signing call sites: (1) the `if sign and verdict.get("verdict") == "PASS"` block in plan_review/__init__.py, and (2) the pre-sign check in transition_close.py before calling _signing.sign_manifest'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the exit-2 contradiction: either clarify that `docs/exit-codes.md` will be amended to document exit 2's dual meaning (usage error AND gate-specific INDETERMINATE), or change the AC so non-retryable INDETERMINATE uses a different exit code — both paths require an explicit direction in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan simultaneously states that `docs/exit-codes.md` is FROZEN with 'exit 2 = usage error, NOT INDETERMINATE' and that non-retryable INDETERMINATE verdicts should continue to exit 2 (AC: 'Plan-review CLI exits 2 (the existing INDETERMINATE code) when the verdict is INDETERMINATE with a non-retryable disposition'). These two statements directly contradict each other: if exit 2 means 'usage error' per the frozen doc, then INDETERMINATE-non-retryable verdicts cannot legitimately exit 2 without amending that doc's meaning.
findings.location: What section (exit-code-11 contract), AC line 'Plan-review CLI exits 2', Verified structural facts ('2=usage/INDETERMINATE' vs 'exit 2 = usage error, NOT INDETERMINATE')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the What section (exit-code-11 contract) explicitly stating: 'The docs/exit-codes.md amendment for exit 11 will also formally record exit 2's dual role (usage error for CLI misuse; INDETERMINATE for plan-review gate failures) as an existing pre-existing behavior, preserving the current pin.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify atomicity of the fingerprint re-check + sign sequence: either move the re-check inside the write lock (so re-check and sign are atomic), or document explicitly why the TOCTOU window is acceptable in this deployment (e.g., the write lock is held by the caller before the re-check is called) — and add a test asserting the ordering and atomicity guarantee.
findings.finding: The plan directs 'Re-check the material fingerprint immediately before signing' AND 'Verify remains OUTSIDE the write lock (ordering preserved)' — these two constraints together create a TOCTOU race: the fingerprint is re-checked outside the lock, then signing happens (inside or acquiring the lock), but between those two steps another process can modify the material, resulting in signing a drifted artifact that passed the re-check.
findings.location: 'Never-sign structural guard' section; AC: 'The material fingerprint is re-checked immediately before signing' and 'Verify remains outside the write lock (ordering preserved; asserted by a test)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State all 8 `resolution_class` enum values inline in this plan (or in a shared constants file this plan references) so the executor can write the JSON Schema enum array without consulting mamba's story.
findings.finding: The plan names 'an enum of the 8 closed values' for `coverage.resolution_class` but never lists those 8 values. An executor writing the JSON Schema `enum` array for all three verdict schemas cannot complete the task without knowing every value — two (WAIT_AND_RETRY, RETRY_NOW) are named inline; the other six are undefined in this plan and only implied to live in mamba's story, which this story's executor may not have access to.
findings.location: What section: 'Verdict-schema field' bullet; AC: 'coverage.resolution_class (enum, 8 closed values) is an optional property on all three verdict schemas'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table/list of all 8 values in the 'Verdict-schema field' bullet, e.g.: `WAIT_AND_RETRY`, `RETRY_NOW`, `PERMANENT_FAILURE`, `CONTENT_POLICY`, `INVALID_RESPONSE`, `TIMEOUT`, `UPSTREAM_ERROR`, `UNKNOWN` — whatever mamba defines — so the schema enum is fully specified here.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback path for the pre-sign fingerprint re-check change (e.g., 'rolling back this story reverts to the prior sign-without-recheck behavior; no data migration needed') so the behavioral change is treated as an explicit justified choice per the compat criterion.
findings.finding: The pre-sign fingerprint re-check ('Re-check the material fingerprint immediately before signing') is described as a deliverable in the What section but has no rollback path stated for the case where the re-check causes a NEW signing failure (content drifted in the verify->sign window). This is a behavioral change to the signing path that could newly block callers that previously succeeded.
findings.location: What section — 'Never-sign structural guard' paragraph; no corresponding rollback statement
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section or the never-sign guard paragraph, add: 'Rollback: revert the fingerprint re-check call; no schema or data migration needed. Consumers that currently sign after drifted content will revert to the prior (weaker) behavior.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement: each degrade site (gate_dispatch.py degrade helpers and transition_close.py catch block) must emit a structured log event at WARNING level with a stable key (e.g., `event='llm_degraded_verdict'`, `gate=...`, `resolution_class=...`) so operators can alert and aggregate on degraded-verdict frequency.
findings.finding: The degraded-verdict path (new exit-11 / block-but-retryable code path across all three gates) introduces no instrumented metric, counter, or alert — operators have no way to observe the rate or pattern of degraded verdicts in production beyond grepping session logs.
findings.location: 'Two-scope posture' and 'Exit-code-11 contract' sections; Acceptance Criteria list; 'What' section throughout

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback statement for the never-sign structural guard to the exit-11/never-sign ADR: specify what operators must do if the guard is reverted (e.g., audit attestation records written during the guard's active window, confirm no degraded verdicts were incorrectly signed pre-guard), and whether the guard can be disabled independently of the exit-11 mapping.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a rollback path for exit-11 ('rollback = revert the exit-11 mapping to the prior INDETERMINATE exit') and for schema fields (additive/optional), but provides no rollback path for the structural never-sign guard: if this guard is rolled back after deployment, any verdicts signed (or rejected) during the rollback window under the new guard semantics leave an inconsistent attestation record with no cleanup or reconciliation step specified.
findings.location: 'Never-sign structural guard' section; AC: 'The structural never-sign guard rejects signing any degraded / INDETERMINATE verdict'; exit-11 rollback under 'Exit-code-11 contract'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Audit the four named ADRs against `docs/adr/`: confirm which (if any) already exist, and for each pre-existing one clarify whether this story AMENDS the existing file or creates a NEW numbered entry — and update the AC and numbering plan accordingly.
findings.finding: Two of the four ADRs the plan says this story will 'record' already exist in `docs/adr/`: `0037-transport-retry.md` and `0038-async-liveness-watchdog.md`. The plan states 'Four ADRs are recorded: exit-11 contract, diagnostic sanitization, deferred async watchdog, transport-retry side-effect-safety architecture' and 'This story RECORDS all 4 ADRs'. Landing duplicate ADR files at the same paths would clobber existing decisions; landing them at new paths would violate the single-numbering convention.
findings.location: What section / Integration ownership / AC row: 'Four ADRs are recorded'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/review_bot/adapter.py` to the consumer enumeration for the `coverage.resolution_class` schema addition, and decide explicitly whether `_coverage_gap_reason()` / `_summarize()` should be updated in this story or deferred.
findings.finding: The Gerrit reviewer-bot adapter (`src/rebar/review_bot/adapter.py`) is an unenumerated consumer of the `coverage.resolution_class` schema addition. `_coverage_gap_reason()` and `_summarize()` currently read `coverage.llm_unavailable` to distinguish outage gaps, but after this story adds `resolution_class` to coverage for degraded verdicts, the adapter receives those verdicts via `produce_code_review_verdict` and its message/tag will carry the old 'llm-unavailable' gap reason regardless of whether the outage was retryable. The plan enumerates CI scripts and the Gerrit reviewer-bot for exit-code consumer impact but does not enumerate the adapter as a consumer of the new schema field.
findings.location: Exit-code-11 contract section / 'enumerate exit-code consumers (CI scripts, the Gerrit reviewer-bot pipeline)'; `src/rebar/review_bot/adapter.py:77-89`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the exit-11 ADR, explicitly note awareness of established 'transient failure' exit-code conventions (e.g. sysexits.h, curl, Nagios) and state why 11 (sequential continuation of rebar's own 0/1/2/10 namespace) is preferred over cross-tool conventions.
findings.finding: Exit code 11 is chosen with no stated rationale for the specific value 11 versus established prior art for 'transient failure' exit codes (sysexits.h has no TEMPFAIL; POSIX-adjacent tools use 75/EX_TEMPFAIL; Nagios uses 3=UNKNOWN; curl uses 28=timeout; LSB init scripts use specific ranges). The plan mandates an ADR but does not require the ADR to demonstrate awareness of these conventions and explain why 11 (next-available sequential slot after 10) is the right choice.
findings.location: What section, 'Exit-code-11 contract' bullet; `docs/exit-codes.md` (Recorded decisions section)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit POST-ROLLBACK SCHEMA STATE assertion: state that after rollback, any on-disk verdict documents carrying `coverage.resolution_class` remain harmless because (a) the rolled-back CLI will treat them as INDETERMINATE (non-retryable) via its existing path and (b) the signing path — even without the structural guard — will not sign them because they will still carry a non-PASS `verdict` field; document this invariant in the rollback section of the exit-11 ADR.
findings.finding: The plan's rollback description ('revert the exit-11 mapping to the prior INDETERMINATE exit') does not assert the POST-ROLLBACK SCHEMA STATE of verdict documents already written with `coverage.resolution_class` present. Specifically: after a rollback removes the never-sign guard (`verdict.coverage.get('resolution_class') is None`), a degraded verdict document already persisted on disk with `resolution_class` set could be re-read by the rolled-back signing path and — lacking the guard — could be signed when it should not be. The plan claims additive-safety for readers that 'ignore unknown keys' but that applies to readers that do nothing special with the field; the never-sign guard is a reader that acts on the field's presence, and its removal on rollback is the exact window where a persisted degraded verdict could slip through to signing.
findings.location: What section — 'Never-sign structural guard' + 'Exit-code-11 contract' rollback note; AC line: 'A degraded / INDETERMINATE verdict is emitted unsigned'
findings.suggested_fix: In the exit-11 ADR rollback section, add: 'Post-rollback, any verdict documents on disk carrying coverage.resolution_class are safe: the rolled-back gate code does not sign INDETERMINATE/degraded verdicts because the verdict.verdict field will be INDETERMINATE (not PASS), which the pre-existing unsigned invariant already blocks. No scrub of persisted documents is required.' If that pre-existing invariant cannot be confirmed, add an explicit compensating guard: 'on rollback, add a temporary filter in sign_manifest that rejects any verdict dict carrying coverage.resolution_class regardless of the structural guard's presence.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
